name: Space Shuttle Challenger
description: The Space Shuttle Challenger disaster, occurring on January 28, 1986, resulted in the deaths of all seven astronauts on board. The official cause, determined by the Rogers Commission, was the failure of an O-ring seal in the right solid rocket booster, exacerbated by unusually cold temperatures. However, the event has spawned several conspiracy theories. Some claim the disaster was an intentional act, possibly due to sabotage, government cover-up, or even a staged event involving faked deaths. Proponents often cite inconsistencies in the official narrative, anomalies in video footage, and supposed connections to broader conspiracies involving NASA or the military-industrial complex. Some theories suggest the astronauts were still alive after the initial explosion, perhaps rescued and reassigned under new identities. The mainstream perspective, supported by extensive scientific investigation and engineering analysis, firmly attributes the disaster to mechanical failure and organizational shortcomings within NASA. Critics of conspiracy theories point to the lack of credible evidence, the reliance on misinterpretations of data, and the emotional motivations of those seeking alternative explanations for a tragic event. The Challenger disaster remains a significant event in American history, science, and culture, prompting ongoing reflection and debate about safety, risk, and the pursuit of technological advancement.
subtitle: A tragic disaster, fertile ground for theories

claims.claim: The Challenger explosion was a deliberate act of sabotage.
claims.evidence: Alleged inconsistencies in video footage, rumors of disgruntled employees, and supposed political motivations.
claims.criticism: Lack of credible evidence linking sabotage to the disaster. The Rogers Commission thoroughly investigated potential sabotage and found no evidence to support it.

claims.claim: The astronauts survived the explosion and were secretly rescued.
claims.evidence: Claims of astronauts seen alive years later, speculation about NASA's capabilities for secret rescues, and interpretations of debris patterns.
claims.criticism: No verifiable evidence of surviving astronauts. The force of the explosion and subsequent impact with the ocean would have been unsurvivable. The debris field was extensively analyzed, confirming the disintegration of the shuttle.

claims.claim: The Challenger disaster was a staged hoax designed to manipulate public opinion or secure funding for NASA.
claims.evidence: Claims of inconsistencies in the timeline of events, alleged manipulation of photographic evidence, and theories about NASA's need for increased funding.
claims.criticism: The evidence for this claim is based on misinterpretations and distortions of facts. The Rogers Commission report and subsequent investigations provide a detailed and well-supported account of the disaster.

keyFigures.name: Richard Feynman
keyFigures.role: Physicist on the Rogers Commission who demonstrated the O-ring failure.

keyFigures.name: Linda Moulton Howe
keyFigures.role: Investigative reporter who has explored alternative theories about the Challenger disaster.

timeline.event: Space Shuttle Challenger explodes 73 seconds after liftoff.
The Rogers Commission releases its report, detailing the causes of the disaster.
Various conspiracy theories emerge and circulate online and in print.

sources.author: Rogers Commission
sources.work: Report of the Presidential Commission on the Space Shuttle Challenger Accident
sources.title: Rogers Commission

sources.author: Curtis Peebles
sources.work: Icarus Fallen: The Search for Meaning in the Face of Forgetting
sources.title: Curtis Peebles
